evidence: withToolWrapper provides a clean, consistent seam for all toolkit tools — handles timing, session.touch(), telemetry, and error wrapping in one place. Every tool function delegates through it with zero glue code.

The publish pipeline has a clear check-then-commit pattern: guards run without mutation first (checkAndRecordWrite with false), pipeline executes, then guards record on success (checkAndRecordWrite with true). Error at any stage means no side effects.

fetchSource centralizes retry, timeout, and rate-limit logic so provider adapters stay pure URL generators. Clean separation of concerns between fetch.ts (HTTP mechanics) and providers/ (URL construction).
Event loop orchestration reads as composition: SourceRegistration configs -> poll -> diff -> filter -> dispatch -> save watermark. Each step is a clean function boundary.
The ActionExecutorContext with 18+ fields creates a wide seam between event-runner.ts and action-executor.ts. While the fields are well-typed, the handoff requires constructing a large object.
Data translation between SDK responses and toolkit types happens in sdk-bridge.ts (decodeHiveData, extractTxHash, normalizeTransaction) — one obvious place for chain data translation.
The sources subsystem has clean layered handoffs: catalog.ts (registry) -> policy.ts (selection) -> fetch.ts (HTTP) -> providers/ (URL/parse). Each boundary is explicit and minimal.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: Module handoffs are largely explicit and well-designed. The toolkit uses a consistent wrapper pattern (withToolWrapper) that makes every tool's seam behavior predictable. The source pipeline (catalog -> policy -> fetch -> providers) demonstrates strong layered composition. The main friction is the wide ActionExecutorContext handoff and the dual publish paths (toolkit publish vs. actions/publish-pipeline) that serve different consumers but share conceptual responsibility.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: A developer crossing module boundaries would mostly find explicit, minimal handoffs — consistent with strong mid-level craft. The withToolWrapper pattern, the check-then-commit guard flow, and the layered source pipeline are all above the 85 threshold individually. The wide ActionExecutorContext and the two publish pipelines (toolkit publish.ts using sdk-bridge for chain ops vs. actions/publish-pipeline.ts using direct SDK for the session runner) introduce boundary friction that keeps the score below 90.

issues.summary: Two separate publish pipelines serve different consumers with partially overlapping logic
evidence: toolkit/tools/publish.ts orchestrates: validate -> guard check -> executePublishPipeline -> guard record, using sdk-bridge for chain operations
actions/publish-pipeline.ts orchestrates: DAHR attestation -> HIVE encoding -> DemosTransactions pipeline, used directly by session-runner
Both handle HIVE post encoding and chain transaction submission but through different SDK access patterns
issues.suggestion: Document the intentional boundary: toolkit/publish.ts is the stable external API (for future @demos-agents/core consumers), while actions/publish-pipeline.ts is the internal session-runner pipeline. Add cross-references between the two files to prevent confusion.

issues.summary: ActionExecutorContext requires constructing an 18+ field object at the event-runner boundary
evidence: The event-runner must wire 18+ fields into ActionExecutorContext: identity (3), auth (1), budget (2), config (3), LLM (1), state (1), platform (4), rate-limiting (3), telemetry (2). This is the widest handoff seam in the codebase.
issues.suggestion: Group related fields into sub-contexts (e.g., AuthContext, BudgetContext, PlatformContext) that can be constructed independently and composed. This makes partial testing easier and the wiring site more readable.

root_causes: The dual publish pipeline exists because the toolkit API (stable external surface) and session-runner (internal orchestrator) evolved independently. The wide ActionExecutorContext is a consequence of extracting the executor for testability without grouping the dependencies.

add.header: withToolWrapper provides consistent tool seam pattern
add.description: Every toolkit tool delegates through withToolWrapper which handles timing, session.touch(), onToolCall telemetry, and error wrapping. This makes seam behavior completely predictable across 10 tools.

add.header: Check-then-commit guard flow prevents partial side effects
add.description: publish.ts runs guards in check-only mode (false), executes the pipeline, then records on success (true). Failure at any step means no guard state mutation — clean transactional semantics at the boundary.

add.header: Source pipeline has clean layered composition
add.description: catalog.ts (registry) -> policy.ts (selection) -> fetch.ts (HTTP) -> providers/ (URL/parse). Each layer has a clear contract and data translation happens at explicit boundaries. No glue-code entropy.

add.header: SDK data translation centralized in sdk-bridge.ts
add.description: All chain data decoding (HIVE payloads, transaction normalization, txHash extraction) happens in one file. Consumers never deal with raw SDK response shapes.
